  3. Using AI Tools for R Coding

Using AI Tools for R Coding

R
BA
reading
A practical overview of how to use AI assistants effectively for R programming tasks — from generating first-draft scripts to debugging — including how to write good prompts and what AI tools cannot do for you.
Author

Claudius Gräbner-Radkowitsch

Published

June 16, 2026

Modified

June 16, 2026

Modern AI tools can meaningfully accelerate R programming work — but only if you already understand the language well enough to evaluate what the AI produces. An AI that generates R code you cannot read is not a shortcut; it is a liability.

With that caveat in mind, there are two use cases where AI assistance is particularly valuable:

  1. Generating a first draft. Given a clear description of what you want to do, an AI tool can produce a working starting point faster than writing from scratch. You then read, understand, and adapt it.
  2. Debugging. Paste code and an error message into an AI assistant, explain what you expected, and it will often identify the problem quickly.

In both cases, providing context is essential. An AI working without context about your data structure, packages, or project conventions will produce generic code that may not fit your situation. The more precisely you describe the problem, the more useful the output.

Video lecture

TipPrompts and code examples from the lecture

1 Tools and resources

Learning to prompt effectively

  • Anthropic’s Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial — a thorough, hands-on course on writing good prompts

Integrating AI into RStudio

  • GitHub Copilot in RStudio — tab-completion and inline suggestions directly in the editor; free for students via GitHub Education
  • Gemini Code Assist — free tier, but no direct RStudio plugin

Standalone AI coding environments

  • Claude Code — best suited for larger programming projects rather than single-file R scripts
  • Windsurf — has a generous free tier but no RStudio integration
NoteA note on reliance

AI tools improve fastest when you treat their output critically. If you paste code you do not understand into your script, you will struggle to adapt it, debug it, or explain it — all of which you will eventually need to do. Use AI as an accelerator, not a replacement for understanding.

Modern AI tools can meaningfully accelerate R programming work — but only if you already
understand the language well enough to evaluate what the AI produces. An AI that generates
R code you cannot read is not a shortcut; it is a liability.

With that caveat in mind, there are two use cases where AI assistance is particularly
valuable:

1. **Generating a first draft.** Given a clear description of what you want to do, an AI
   tool can produce a working starting point faster than writing from scratch. You then read,
   understand, and adapt it.
2. **Debugging.** Paste code and an error message into an AI assistant, explain what you
   expected, and it will often identify the problem quickly.

In both cases, providing context is essential. An AI working without context about your
data structure, packages, or project conventions will produce generic code that may not
fit your situation. The more precisely you describe the problem, the more useful the output.

## Prompts and code examples from the lecture

:::

# Tools and resources

**Learning to prompt effectively**

- [Anthropic's Prompt Engineering Interactive Tutorial](https://github.com/anthropics/prompt-eng-interactive-tutorial) — a thorough, hands-on course on writing good prompts

**Integrating AI into RStudio**

- [GitHub Copilot in RStudio](https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/ide/guide/tools/copilot.html) — tab-completion and inline suggestions directly in the editor; free for students via [GitHub Education](https://github.com/education/students)
- [Gemini Code Assist](https://codeassist.google) — free tier, but no direct RStudio plugin

**Standalone AI coding environments**

- [Claude Code](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview) — best suited for larger programming projects rather than single-file R scripts
- [Windsurf](https://windsurf.com) — has a generous free tier but no RStudio integration

::: {.callout-note}
## A note on reliance

AI tools improve fastest when you treat their output critically. If you paste code you
do not understand into your script, you will struggle to adapt it, debug it, or explain
it — all of which you will eventually need to do. Use AI as an accelerator, not a
replacement for understanding.
:::
